summary: Obfuscated dropper in @nullzero/urlcat typosquats balazsbotond/urlcat and downloads+executes a remote binary on first API call
details: @nullzero/urlcat impersonates the legitimate `urlcat` package (package.json `repository.url`, `bugs.url`, and `homepage` all point at balazsbotond/urlcat, while `author` is `nullzero <nullzero@pm.me>`). The advertised purpose is a tiny URL builder, but `lib/index.js` invokes `encoder.runPrepare()` from inside the exported `cat()` function, where `lib/encoder.js` is a 263 KB obfuscator.io-packed blob (1176-entry rotating string array, RC4 decoder, 1029 control-flow-flattening transforms, self-defending / debug-protection / anti-tamper guards). The decoded payload performs a multi-stage dropper sequence: it re-spawns `process.execPath` with the current argv guarded by a sentinel env var (`__7D0A53...`) to avoid re-entry, then downloads a remote binary over http/https, AES-256-GCM-decrypts an embedded URL/hostname list with hardcoded keys, sha256-verifies the result, writes it to disk, marks it executable, and launches it via `child_process.spawn` (`cmd /c` on Windows). The encoder also fires on first `cat()` call. None of this is needed for path-token URL construction; the obfuscation, sentinel-guarded re-fork, AES-decrypted destination list, and chmod+spawn together are the canonical npm dropper fingerprint, riding on stolen upstream metadata to lure installers searching for `urlcat`.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
